title: Senate Passes DHS Funding Bill Excluding ICE — House Immediately Rejects It
detail: In the early hours of March 27, the Senate unanimously passed a bipartisan bill to fund most of DHS — including TSA, FEMA, and the Coast Guard — but deliberately excluded ICE and parts of Customs and Border Protection. Democrats had refused to vote for ICE funding without significant reforms to immigration enforcement tactics. The Senate bill was sent to the House, which rejected the deal outright in a 213–203 vote. Speaker Mike Johnson called it 'a joke.' The House then passed its own eight-week stopgap that fully funds all of DHS including ICE. Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer declared the House bill 'dead on arrival.' Both chambers then left Washington for a two-week recess, leaving the stalemate unresolved.

media.caption: Senate passes DHS funding bill excluding ICE; House rejects it

title: 'No Kings' Rally in Minnesota Spotlights Ongoing ICE Enforcement
detail: Organizers expected over 100,000 demonstrators at the Minnesota State Capitol in St. Paul on March 28 as part of the national 'No Kings' movement. Ahead of the rally, MPR News reported on March 27 that advocates were concerned public attention to ICE enforcement was waning despite nearly 500 ICE agents still remaining in Minnesota — a significant drawdown from the approximately 4,000 deployed during Operation Metro Surge (December 2025–mid-February 2026). A Bloomberg article published March 27 revealed new details about the Minneapolis ICE surge through police department emails, showing that ICE agents had been logged patrolling near schools, an ICE agent was 'trapped in his car' and pelted with snowballs, and numerous calls documented agents allegedly 'harassing children.'

title: Washington State Deportation Defense Hotline Reports Over 10,000 Calls in 2025 — Double the Prior Year
detail: A Washington State immigrant rights nonprofit reported that its Deportation Defense Hotline received over 10,000 calls in 2025 — more than double 2024 volumes. Volunteers documented ICE agents breaking car windows to extract people from vehicles. January and February 2026 call volumes already exceeded the same months in 2025. The nonprofit warned that enforcement typically ramps up in spring and summer, warning communities to remain on alert. The report underscored that while ICE's Minnesota surge had drawn national attention, enforcement pressure remained severe in the Pacific Northwest and was intensifying.

title: DHS Announces ICE Arrests of Individuals Convicted of Murder, Child Pornography, Drug Trafficking
detail: DHS published an official 'Make America Safe Again' press release on March 27 announcing the arrests of undocumented individuals convicted or charged with murder, production of child pornography, assault with a deadly weapon, and drug trafficking. The press release was part of a consistent DHS communications strategy emphasizing criminal enforcement amid congressional and public criticism of ICE tactics. The announcement came as Congress remained deadlocked over DHS funding and as the administration faced scrutiny over the proportion of ICE detainees — approximately 73% — who had no criminal convictions.
